text.content:
Clock 1 — Route-Cleansing Clock

Six segments. Tracks the enemy actively destroying or altering evidence. This is the phase's main pressure. The enemy is no longer trying to outwit the party; they are racing the party to delete the route from history.

SegmentState change
0 — InitialDefault. Iren's shop is intact. Vone still working. Korre's apartment was stripped at the end of Phase 6 if she escaped; otherwise still on its way to being stripped.
1 — Korre's address is strippedThe Tannerway apartment is being scrubbed by two strangers; the residue is gone within 12 hours. If the party hasn't visited yet, they lose the ledger line and wax flake unless they recovered them in Phase 6.
2 — Kestrel & Reed lockbox records are alteredBox 17 has been emptied. Doras Kemmel (if not turned) has been told to forget the account. The forwarding chits are gone.
3 — Vone is warned or vanishesVone's reception is locked. His prices double. By segment 4 he has fled to the Bracken Lane cellar.
4 — One old mining map is stolen or replacedThe Guild archive's old map of the Tarnsmere Spur is replaced with a clean modern map. Yorth Pell (the Guild underclerk) is paid extra for the swap.
5 — The Seven-Cut Cairn waymark is defacedA route-cleanser has reached the cairn before the party. The seventh cut is scraped almost flat. The party can still find the site — but they will need Vetch's sketched map (Handout 5) or Aldea's material analysis to confirm it.
6 — The threshold is sealed, trapped, or guardedThe party arrives at the Seven-Cut Cairn and finds it actively defended. The ruined tollhouse is occupied by two route-cleansers and a hired tough. The sealed stairwell beneath has been re-stoned. Phase 8 starts with the party fighting their way to the threshold.

What advances Clock 1

  • Using public authority loudly (+1 each public showing).
  • Asking about Seventh Stair by name in a public archive (+1).
  • Threatening Vone or Iren in public (+1).
  • Killing or displaying a captured agent (+2).
  • Delaying more than a full in-game day between major leads (+1 per delay).
  • Allowing a prisoner to be seen by Greyhawk authorities (+1).
  • Confronting Guild records openly with Yeomanry authority (+1).
  • Taking false evidence at face value (Node 6 fail) (+1).
  • Carryover from Phase 6: if Opening D applied, start at 1; if Opening G applied, start at 2.

What does not advance Clock 1

  • Private consultation with Iren, Aldea, Vetch, or by-courier correspondence with Caelith.
  • Bribery if done quietly.
  • Stealth approaches that leave no signs.
  • Single-PC visits to a node, especially if the PC arrives and leaves separately from the rest.

Splitting the party intelligently

As with Phase 6, splitting the party in well-considered ways reduces clock advancement. A PC at Iren's, a PC at Vetch's house, and a PC at Vone's, on the same afternoon, produces no pattern visible to the conspiracy. Sending all six PCs to Vone's in matching armor produces a great deal of pattern.

Clock 2 — Prisoner / Heat Clock

Six segments. Use this clock if Haskur, Kethren, Korre, or another key figure was captured in Phase 6. It tracks the legal and social pressure that builds around a held prisoner in Greyhawk City.

If no prisoner: the clock measures general Greyhawk Heat instead. Carry forward the Phase 6 Heat Clock state as the starting segment.

SegmentState change
0 — InitialDefault. The party has a private room and a private prisoner. No one outside the party knows.
1 — Local authorities ask jurisdictional questionsA Greyhawk City Watch officer (Captain Heshet from Phase 6) calls at the party's lodging. He is polite. He asks who the prisoner is. He does not press.
2 — A legal claimant appearsAn Aerdy commercial advocate, or a Greyhawk advocate retained by an unknown client, files a formal inquiry. The party is given 48 hours to respond.
3 — A forged custody order arrivesA document arrives demanding transfer of the prisoner. It looks authentic. Investigation DC 15 reveals subtle forgery. If the party complies, the prisoner is taken; if not, the order escalates.
4 — The prisoner is targeted for silence or extractionAn extraction attempt is made: poison in the prisoner's water, a bribed servant, a quiet rooftop intrusion. The party must defend the prisoner. This is the only required combat scene in Phase 7.
5 — Greyhawk Watch pressure escalatesCaptain Heshet returns formally. The party is told that any visible movement of the prisoner draws immediate detention. The prisoner cannot be moved without arrangement.
6 — The prisoner must be moved, released, hidden, or publicly surrenderedThe party has 24 hours to choose. Each option has consequences carried into Phase 8.

What advances Clock 2

  • Allowing the prisoner to be seen in public (+2).
  • Using public authority around the prisoner (+1).
  • Threatening or harming the prisoner visibly (+1 per incident).
  • Trying to move the prisoner across the city in daylight (+1).
  • Caelith intervening publicly to claim jurisdiction (+1; only if Closure didn't pass).
  • One day passes with the prisoner held (+1 per day after the second).

Carryover from Phase 6

If the party caused open violence at the Old Stonecistern (e.g., Korre's agents killed in a fight), start the Prisoner Clock at 1. If the party killed Haskur visibly, start at 1. If both, start at 2.

The prisoner as resource and liability

A prisoner gives the party access to a Phase 7 evidence category they otherwise couldn't reach (see Phase 7 — Primary Nodes — Node 5). They also commit the party to a defensive posture in Greyhawk. The right answer is usually to extract intelligence quickly and arrange a quiet hand-off to Caelith — if the party hesitates, the clock catches them.

The required combat (segment 4 extraction attempt)

The only Phase 7 combat that is essentially mandatory. Use:

  • 1 silent scout (Scout with poison needle, escape tools).
  • 1 record-burner (Cult Fanatic or Mage-lite analog) — here, an arcane operative, not a religious figure.
  • 2 hired toughs (Thug analogs).

The extraction party arrives quietly at the third bell after midnight. Their goal is the prisoner. They are not trying to kill the PCs — they are trying to move silently and leave silently. Detection (passive Perception 14) catches the intrusion early; otherwise the alarm goes up only when the prisoner is touched.

If the party fails: the prisoner is gone by morning. The Route-Cleansing Clock advances by 1. The party's investigation continues from the documents and witnesses they've collected.

Optional Enemy Operations

The conspiracy is no longer interested in killing the party. They are interested in deleting the route. Choose one or several of these operations to run in parallel with the party's investigation, particularly if the Route-Cleansing Clock is advancing slowly.

On-brand for this conspiracy: deletion, alteration, misdirection, and retrieval. Avoid generic assassination — that would be out of character. Stationery-cult energy.

A. Route-cleanser team defaces waymarks

Run alongside Node 7 (field reconnaissance). Two cleansers in masons' aprons working their way along the Tarnsmere Spur, scoring the seventh cuts off the stone markers. The party can intercept them mid-work (one optional encounter; see Node 7 option C).

B. False map ambush at wrong quarry

See Node 6. A planted false map leads the party to a dry quarry shaft northwest of the city. Two cleansers, a hired tough, and an optional smoke or timber trap. The party leaves angrier.

C. Prisoner extraction

See Clock 2 segment 4. The required combat scene of the phase. Happens automatically if a prisoner is held more than three days.

D. Witness pressure against Dalmor Vetch

A Guild “safety officer” calls at Vetch's house and informs him that his pension is being “reviewed.” The pressure is light but specific. Vetch refuses to talk to the party for 48 hours unless they offer him an alternative income or formal protection from the Guild.

E. Aldea's packet intercepted and copied

If Lie 2 was active in Phase 6, this happens automatically. Otherwise it happens at Route-Cleansing Clock 3. Aldea discovers the interception by the third day. She tells the party. The party should respond; if not, the conspiracy gets a copy of her material analysis, and the Route-Cleansing Clock advances by 1.

F. Guild record substituted with a cleaner false copy

Happens at Route-Cleansing Clock 4. The Tarnsmere Spur file in the Guild archive is replaced by a clean modern copy with the cairn waymark removed. The party can recover from this by:

  • Bringing in Vetch as a witness (his memory predates the substitution).
  • Cross-referencing with the old map's known errata (Investigation DC 16 by the party scholar).
  • Recovering the original from Yorth Pell, who kept a private copy before swapping it (Persuasion 17 or Intimidation 18).

The recall threat (Kethren only)

If Kethren is captured, the conspiracy will at some point try to recall him. This is a Phase 7 plot beat, not a mechanic the party can directly engage with. The recall manifests as Kethren growing increasingly afraid — staring at his hands, refusing to eat, asking once not to be returned — over the course of a week. By the end of the week, if the party has not provided some form of protection, Kethren is simply gone from his locked room. The door is still locked. He is gone.

Phase 7 does not explain how this happened. Phase 8 may begin to.

Faction reactions in Phase 7

FactionDefault dispositionShifts hostile if…
Greyhawk Guild of Miners & JewelersCordial, slowYeomanry authority brought publicly; Vetch named.
Greyhawk City WatchIndifferentVisible prisoner; visible violence; Heat Clock 3+.
Caelith's officeDistant allyClosure passed already pre-shifts to writing-only.
Cindren & Vhal residualQuiet, watchfulPath C carryover.
Brane / Closure faction (Loftwick)Distant adversaryPublic Greyhawk authority gives them fuel back home.
Aerdy commercial advocatesProcedural, expensiveIf Sereth was Path C; if Path A, Halask remains active.
Route-cleansersHostile-on-contactAlways hostile; will flee before dying.
